observations: ct-select renders no images. The dropdown chevron is a CSS background-image (`--ct-select-*-icon`), a decorative affordance rather than content needing a text alternative.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: The select's accessible name depends on a programmatic label association, but `ct-select` renders its real `<select>` inside a shadow root — an external `<label for="...">` in the implementer's light DOM cannot cross that shadow boundary to associate with the internal control, and wrapping doesn't help either since the labelable descendant lives in a different shadow tree. Implementers must set `aria-label` on `ct-select` (forwarded directly onto the internal `<select>`) instead of relying on native `for`/`id` or wrapping. Option grouping (`ct-select-optgroup`'s `label`) is structurally correct via a real `<optgroup>`, independent of this caveat.

For selects collecting a recognized input purpose (e.g. country), an `autocomplete` attribute should be set — `ct-select`'s Lit properties do not currently expose one (matching upstream's `select.component.yml`, which also has no such field), so today this depends entirely on whether an implementer's own tooling can attach it; the component itself has no passthrough for arbitrary attributes.

The `invalid` state changes border/background color, but it also sets `aria-invalid="true"` on the underlying `<select>` — a non-color signal. Full compliance for a real validation error still depends on the implementer pairing this with visible, programmatically-associated error text (e.g. `ct-field-message`) rather than relying on the color change alone.

Text/border colors are sourced directly from CivicTheme's own design tokens (`--ct-select-*-color`, `--ct-select-*-border-color`) rather than approximated, but this port does not independently re-verify the computed contrast ratio for every light/dark/hover/focus/invalid state combination.
Typography uses relative (rem) units throughout (`--ct-typography-label-small-*`); text remains visible and readable at 200% zoom.

Border-color tokens for the default/hover/focus/invalid states are sourced from CivicTheme's own palette, but this port does not independently re-verify the required 3:1 contrast against the adjacent background for every theme/state combination.
Text remains visible/readable when line spacing, letter spacing, and word spacing are increased per this criterion's thresholds — no fixed-height container clips the option text.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)
observations: The native option list shown on activation is browser-rendered chrome, not additional content this component introduces or controls.
Native `<select>` semantics provide full keyboard operability (arrow keys to cycle options, typeahead, Enter/Space to open/close) without any custom scripting.
The native control does not trap focus; Escape/Tab move focus away normally.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: The control participates in the natural document/tab order; no tabindex overrides are applied.
ct-select renders no links.
Compliance depends on the implementer supplying clear, descriptive `ct-select-option`/`ct-select-optgroup` `label` values and a clear `aria-label` for the select itself (see 1.3.1).
A visible `:focus-visible` outline (`--ct-select-*-outline-*` tokens) renders on the native control in both themes.

ct-select renders no visible label of its own (see 1.3.1); the `aria-label` an implementer sets must contain the visible label text a sighted user sees rendered elsewhere on the page (e.g. via `ct-label`), or a speech-input user targeting the visible label won't reach this control.

The select's own padding (0.8125rem top/bottom) plus its label-small line-height (1.25rem) give the trigger a computed height around 46px, well over the 24x24 CSS pixel minimum. The native option list's own row sizing is browser/OS-controlled and outside this component's styling scope.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting the correct `lang` attribute on the `<html>` element.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting `lang` attributes on option text that differs from the page's main language.
This component's own implementation triggers no automatic context change (no auto-submit, no navigation) on focus.

ct-select itself wires no `change` handler and triggers no context change on selection, but selects are commonly (mis)used as navigation menus that jump the page on change — a real, select-specific anti-pattern. Compliance depends entirely on what the implementer attaches to the underlying `change` event.
Compliance depends on the implementer using the same option label/value mapping and the same `aria-label` for equivalent selects everywhere they recur across the site.

The `invalid` prop toggles `aria-invalid="true"` and the invalid visual state on the underlying `<select>`, but `ct-select` does not itself render or associate error text — pairing this state with a programmatically-associated `ct-field-message` (or equivalent) describing the specific error is the implementer's responsibility.

ct-select renders no visible label/instructions of its own (see 1.3.1) — providing one, and giving this component an accessible name via `aria-label` (native `for`/`id`/wrapping do not work across its shadow boundary), is the implementer's responsibility.
Compliance depends on the implementer providing a specific, actionable error suggestion alongside the `invalid` state (see 3.3.1), not just the visual/aria-invalid signal alone.

Native `<select>`/`<option>`/`<optgroup>` semantics correctly expose role and state (`aria-invalid`, `disabled`, `required`, selected values, multiple) automatically. The accessible *name* is only reliably set via the `aria-label` this component forwards onto its internal `<select>` — see 1.3.1 for why native `for`/`id`/wrapping cannot reach across the shadow-DOM boundary the way they can for a plain `<select>`.
Selecting an option produces no status message on its own; if an implementer wires a live-updating result region to selection, exposing it via `aria-live` is their responsibility.
